intro: A five-course exploration of the allium family — ramps, spring garlic, vidalia onions, shallots, and more — at the peak of their spring season. Each course is built around a different preparation and a different member of the family.

courses.name: Ramp Tart
courses.tableside: A one-bite amuse to open the menu. Whipped ramp fromage in a tart shell, finished with trout roe. The ramp is at the center — garlicky, green, and intensely seasonal. The roe adds salinity and a clean finish.

shellfish.note: Trout roe is fish roe, not shellfish. Safe for shellfish allergies.

dairy.note: Fromage contains dairy. Cannot be removed.

gluten.note: Tart shell contains gluten. Cannot be made GF.

eggs.note: Tart shell and fromage may contain eggs.

vegetarian.note: Trout roe can be removed to make vegetarian.

key_terms.definition: A wild spring onion (Allium tricoccum) native to eastern North America, foraged rather than farmed, and available for only a few weeks each spring. The flavor is a cross between garlic and green onion, intensely aromatic and more complex than either. The entire plant is edible — bulb, stem, and leaf.
French for cheese. Used here to describe a whipped, spreadable fresh cheese preparation made from or infused with ramps. Light, creamy, and savory.
The cured eggs of trout. Smaller than salmon roe, with a delicate briny pop and a cleaner, less assertive flavor. A lighter finishing element than sturgeon caviar.

sourcing.name: Ramps
sourcing.description: Foraged from the Appalachian region. One of the first wild edibles to appear after winter, with a peak season of three to five weeks in spring. The short window makes them a genuine seasonal event.

courses.chef_notes: One bite. Set the table for the menu by briefly describing the allium theme before it arrives. Ramp season is short — this dish exists for a few weeks only.

courses.name: French Onion Bruschetta
courses.description: Caramelized vidalia onions, black garlic soubise, gruyere crisp
courses.tableside: The French onion soup concept reimagined as a bruschetta. Vidalia onions caramelized low and slow until deeply sweet, a black garlic soubise underneath, and a gruyere crisp on top. Everything you expect from French onion, in a format you can eat with your hands.

dairy.note: Soubise and gruyere contain dairy. Cannot be removed.

gluten.note: Bruschetta base contains gluten. Cannot be made GF.

eggs.note: Soubise may contain eggs. Confirm with kitchen.

key_terms.definition: A sweet onion variety grown in a specific region of Georgia, where the low sulfur content of the soil produces onions with notably less pungency and more natural sweetness than standard yellow onions. One of the few vegetables with a federally protected geographic designation in the US.

Whole garlic heads fermented at low heat and high humidity for several weeks until the cloves turn black and develop a deeply complex, molasses-like sweetness with hints of balsamic and tamarind. All the sharpness of raw garlic is gone — what remains is mellow and umami-rich.

A classic French sauce made from onions cooked down in butter and cream until completely soft, then pureed. Named after the Maréchal de Soubise, an 18th-century French nobleman. Silky, sweet, and rich.
A firm Swiss cow's milk cheese with a rich, slightly nutty flavor that melts exceptionally well. The classic choice for French onion soup and its natural companion to caramelized onions.

courses.chef_notes: The bruschetta format means this is finger food. Worth mentioning so guests are prepared.

pairing.name: Semillon/Chenin Blanc/Pedro Gimenez, Mendoza 2024
pairing.note: An unusual Argentine white blend with an orchard-fruit nose, elevated floral highlights, and a soft, lively palate finishing on a round note sharpened by tangy acidity. The wine's orchard fruit and floral character sit alongside the sweetness of the caramelized vidalia onions, while the tangy acidity cuts through the richness of the gruyere and black garlic soubise. A white with enough aromatic complexity to hold against a dish that could overwhelm a simpler pour.

courses.name: Bucatini Pasta & Allium Ragu
courses.description: Ramp greens, spring garlic, morel mushroom, chicken liver sausage, brie fonduta, pickled ramps
courses.tableside: Rich braised pasta with layers of allium. The brie fonduta melts into the sauce, the pickled ramps cut through it, and the morel mushroom and chicken liver sausage give the dish its depth and weight. One of the most complex courses on the menu.

dairy.note: Brie fonduta contains dairy. Cannot be removed.

gluten.note: Bucatini is wheat pasta. Cannot be made GF.

key_terms.definition: A thick spaghetti-like pasta with a hollow center running through it. The hollow captures sauce differently than solid pasta, and the extra thickness holds up to a heavy braise like this ragu.
Garlic harvested before the bulb has fully formed, when the plant is still young and the flavor is milder and more herbal than cured garlic. The entire stalk is usable, similar to a green onion.

A wild spring mushroom with a distinctive honeycomb cap and a deep, earthy, nutty flavor. One of the most prized foraged ingredients in American cooking, with a very short spring season. Cannot be cultivated commercially.
An Italian fondue made from melted cheese with milk or cream. Brie fonduta is silkier and more delicate than a Swiss-style fondue — it becomes a sauce rather than a thick dip.

sourcing.name: Morel Mushrooms
sourcing.description: Foraged wild mushrooms available for a short window in spring. The flavor is impossible to replicate with cultivated varieties — deeply savory, earthy, and complex. Their presence on a menu is a genuine seasonal marker.

courses.chef_notes: The chicken liver sausage is house-made. Worth mentioning to guests who ask about the depth of flavor in the ragu.

pairing.name: Valpolicella Ripasso Marogne DOC 2023
pairing.note: A Ripasso made by re-passing standard Valpolicella over the dried skins from Amarone production, picking up extra tannin, color, and concentration without the full weight of Amarone itself. Zeni's version is vinous and full-bodied, with dark cherry, dried herbs, and a rounded, velvety finish. The dried cherry and rounded tannin absorb the richness of the brie fonduta while the wine's acidity sharpens the pickled ramps. The chicken liver sausage needs the weight of a second-pass wine — a standard Valpolicella wouldn't hold here.

courses.name: Spring Lamb Striploin
courses.description: Spring onion whipped potatoes, garlic asparagus, ramp gremolata
courses.tableside: Lamb striploin roasted to order, served with spring onion whipped potatoes and garlic asparagus, finished with a ramp gremolata. The gremolata ties the whole menu together — ramp, parsley, lemon — bright and herbal against the richness of the lamb.

dairy.note: Whipped potatoes contain butter and cream. Can potentially be modified — confirm with kitchen.

key_terms.definition: A lean, tender cut from the short loin of the animal, with a firm texture and clean, direct flavor. Less fatty than a ribeye, more flavorful than a tenderloin. Lamb striploin is the rack equivalent without the bone.

An Italian condiment made from fresh herb, garlic, and lemon zest. The classic version uses parsley. This version substitutes ramp, keeping the preparation within the menu's theme while adding the garlic note the ramp naturally provides.
A young onion harvested before the bulb fully matures. Milder and sweeter than a full onion, with a delicate flavor that holds up well when cooked into whipped potatoes without overpowering the dish.

courses.chef_notes: Ask kitchen for preferred temperature on the lamb. Strong pairing opportunity — the Bordeaux is worth describing in detail for guests who order the pairing.

pairing.name: Merlot/Cab Sauv/Petit Verdot, Listrac-Médoc 2016
pairing.note: A Listrac-Médoc from the 2016 vintage — widely considered one of the great Bordeaux years of the century — now a decade old and drinking at its best. Clean blackberry and bilberry fruit, a light estuarine character that emerges with time, medium body with crunchy dark fruit tinged with tobacco and sea salt, and a vivid finish of black olive. The structure of the Bordeaux holds the lamb striploin firmly, and the ramp gremolata's brightness finds an echo in the wine's herbal edge and acidity. Classic Bordeaux logic with a spring dish.

courses.name: Caramelized Shallot Cake
courses.description: Onion-apple caramel, blue cheese ice cream
courses.tableside: A savory-leaning dessert that closes the allium menu on its own terms. Caramelized shallot cake with onion-apple caramel and blue cheese ice cream. The blue cheese reads as an unexpected but deliberate choice — funky against sweet, cold against warm. It works.

dairy.note: Blue cheese ice cream contains dairy. Cannot be removed.

gluten.note: Cake contains gluten. Cannot be made GF.

eggs.note: Cake and ice cream base contain eggs.

key_terms.definition: A member of the allium family, smaller and more delicate than a standard onion, with a flavor that blends onion sweetness with a hint of garlic. When caramelized, the natural sugars concentrate into a deep, complex sweetness that works unusually well in a dessert context.
Sugar cooked past the melting point until it develops complex bitter, nutty, and toasty flavors. The onion-apple version adds savory and fruit elements that keep the caramel from being purely sweet.

Ice cream made with blue cheese incorporated into the base. The funky, salty character of the blue cuts through the sweetness of the caramel and cake, and the cold temperature contrasts with the warmth of the baked element.

courses.chef_notes: The savory-sweet character of this dessert may need a brief setup for guests who aren't expecting it. Lead with the shallot caramel before mentioning the blue cheese ice cream.

pairing.name: Cream Sherry NV
pairing.note: A Cream Sherry from Jerez — Palomino and Pedro Ximénez blended and aged in the solera system, producing a wine with dried fig, salty caramel, Christmas cake spice, cloves, cinnamon, walnut character, and a soufflé-like finish. Not a wine on our regular list — this bottle is exclusive to the tasting menu pairing. The oxidative, nutty character of the sherry runs alongside the caramelized shallot directly, and the Pedro Ximénez sweetness finds the apple caramel without tipping into excess. The blue cheese ice cream and the salty caramel are the details that make this pairing genuinely interesting.
